scene_title: Elsinore. A platform before the castle

lines.speaker: BERNARDO
lines.text: Who’s there?

lines.speaker: FRANCISCO
lines.text: Nay, answer me: stand, and unfold yourself.

lines.speaker: BERNARDO
lines.text: Long live the king!

lines.speaker: FRANCISCO
lines.text: Bernardo?

lines.speaker: BERNARDO
lines.text: He.

lines.speaker: FRANCISCO
lines.text: You come most carefully upon your hour.

lines.speaker: BERNARDO
lines.text: ’Tis now struck twelve; get thee to bed, Francisco.

lines.speaker: FRANCISCO
lines.text: For this relief much thanks: ’tis bitter cold,

lines.speaker: FRANCISCO
lines.text: And I am sick at heart.

lines.speaker: BERNARDO
lines.text: Have you had quiet guard?

lines.speaker: FRANCISCO
lines.text: Not a mouse stirring.

lines.speaker: BERNARDO
lines.text: Well, good night.

lines.speaker: BERNARDO
lines.text: If you do meet Horatio and Marcellus,

lines.speaker: BERNARDO
lines.text: The rivals of my watch, bid them make haste.

lines.speaker: FRANCISCO
lines.text: I think I hear them. Stand, ho! Who’s there?

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: Friends to this ground.

lines.speaker: MARCELLUS
lines.text: And liegemen to the Dane.

lines.speaker: FRANCISCO
lines.text: Give you good night.

lines.speaker: MARCELLUS
lines.text: O, farewell, honest soldier:

lines.speaker: MARCELLUS
lines.text: Who hath relieved you?

lines.speaker: FRANCISCO
lines.text: Bernardo has my place.

lines.speaker: FRANCISCO
lines.text: Give you good night.

lines.speaker: MARCELLUS
lines.text: Holla! Bernardo!

lines.speaker: BERNARDO
lines.text: Say,

lines.speaker: BERNARDO
lines.text: What, is Horatio there?

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: A piece of him.

lines.speaker: BERNARDO
lines.text: Welcome, Horatio: welcome, good Marcellus.

lines.speaker: MARCELLUS
lines.text: What, has this thing appear’d again to-night?

lines.speaker: BERNARDO
lines.text: I have seen nothing.

lines.speaker: MARCELLUS
lines.text: Horatio says ’tis but our fantasy,

lines.speaker: MARCELLUS
lines.text: And will not let belief take hold of him

lines.speaker: MARCELLUS
lines.text: Touching this dreaded sight, twice seen of us:

lines.speaker: MARCELLUS
lines.text: Therefore I have entreated him along

lines.speaker: MARCELLUS
lines.text: With us to watch the minutes of this night;

lines.speaker: MARCELLUS
lines.text: That if again this apparition come,

lines.speaker: MARCELLUS
lines.text: He may approve our eyes and speak to it.

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: Tush, tush, ’twill not appear.

lines.speaker: BERNARDO
lines.text: Sit down awhile;

lines.speaker: BERNARDO
lines.text: And let us once again assail your ears,

lines.speaker: BERNARDO
lines.text: That are so fortified against our story

lines.speaker: BERNARDO
lines.text: What we have two nights seen.

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: Well, sit we down,

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: And let us hear Bernardo speak of this.

lines.speaker: BERNARDO
lines.text: Last night of all,

lines.speaker: BERNARDO
lines.text: When yond same star that’s westward from the pole

lines.speaker: BERNARDO
lines.text: Had made his course to illume that part of heaven

lines.speaker: BERNARDO
lines.text: Where now it burns, Marcellus and myself,

lines.speaker: BERNARDO
lines.text: The bell then beating one—

lines.speaker: MARCELLUS
lines.text: Peace, break thee off; look, where it comes again!

lines.speaker: BERNARDO
lines.text: In the same figure, like the king that’s dead.

lines.speaker: MARCELLUS
lines.text: Thou art a scholar; speak to it, Horatio.

lines.speaker: BERNARDO
lines.text: Looks it not like the king? mark it, Horatio.

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: Most like: it harrows me with fear and wonder.

lines.speaker: BERNARDO
lines.text: It would be spoke to.

lines.speaker: MARCELLUS
lines.text: Question it, Horatio.

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: What art thou that usurp’st this time of night,

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: Together with that fair and warlike form

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: In which the majesty of buried Denmark

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: Did sometimes march? by heaven I charge thee, speak!

lines.speaker: MARCELLUS
lines.text: It is offended.

lines.speaker: BERNARDO
lines.text: See, it stalks away!

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: Stay! speak, speak! I charge thee, speak!

lines.speaker: MARCELLUS
lines.text: ’Tis gone, and will not answer.

lines.speaker: BERNARDO
lines.text: How now, Horatio! you tremble and look pale:

lines.speaker: BERNARDO
lines.text: Is not this something more than fantasy?

lines.speaker: BERNARDO
lines.text: What think you on’t?

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: Before my God, I might not this believe

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: Without the sensible and true avouch

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: Of mine own eyes.

lines.speaker: MARCELLUS
lines.text: Is it not like the king?

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: As thou art to thyself:

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: Such was the very armour he had on

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: When he the ambitious Norway combated;

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: So frown’d he once, when, in an angry parle,

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: He smote the sledded Polacks on the ice.

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: ’Tis strange.

lines.speaker: MARCELLUS
lines.text: Thus twice before, and jump at this dead hour,

lines.speaker: MARCELLUS
lines.text: With martial stalk hath he gone by our watch.

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: In what particular thought to work I know not;

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: But in the gross and scope of my opinion,

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: This bodes some strange eruption to our state.

lines.speaker: MARCELLUS
lines.text: Good now, sit down, and tell me, he that knows,

lines.speaker: MARCELLUS
lines.text: Why this same strict and most observant watch

lines.speaker: MARCELLUS
lines.text: So nightly toils the subject of the land,

lines.speaker: MARCELLUS
lines.text: And why such daily cast of brazen cannon,

lines.speaker: MARCELLUS
lines.text: And foreign mart for implements of war;

lines.speaker: MARCELLUS
lines.text: Why such impress of shipwrights, whose sore task

lines.speaker: MARCELLUS
lines.text: Does not divide the Sunday from the week;

lines.speaker: MARCELLUS
lines.text: What might be toward, that this sweaty haste

lines.speaker: MARCELLUS
lines.text: Doth make the night joint-labourer with the day:

lines.speaker: MARCELLUS
lines.text: Who is’t that can inform me?

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: That can I;

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: At least, the whisper goes so. Our last king,

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: Whose image even but now appear’d to us,

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: Was, as you know, by Fortinbras of Norway,

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: Thereto prick’d on by a most emulate pride,

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: Dared to the combat; in which our valiant Hamlet—

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: For so this side of our known world esteem’d him—

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: Did slay this Fortinbras; who, by a seal’d compact,

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: Well ratified by law and heraldry,

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: Did forfeit, with his life, all those his lands

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: Which he stood seized of, to the conqueror:

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: Against the which, a moiety competent

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: Was gaged by our king; which had return’d

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: To the inheritance of Fortinbras,

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: Had he been vanquisher; as, by the same covenant,

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: And carriage of the article design’d,

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: His fell to Hamlet. Now, sir, young Fortinbras,

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: Of unimproved mettle hot and full,

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: Hath in the skirts of Norway here and there

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: Shark’d up a list of lawless resolutes,

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: For food and diet, to some enterprise

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: That hath a stomach in’t; which is no other—

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: As it doth well appear unto our state—

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: But to recover of us, by strong hand

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: And terms compulsatory, those foresaid lands

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: So by his father lost: and this, I take it,

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: Is the main motive of our preparations,

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: The source of this our watch and the chief head

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: Of this post-haste and romage in the land.

lines.speaker: BERNARDO
lines.text: I think it be no other but e’en so:

lines.speaker: BERNARDO
lines.text: Well may it sort that this portentous figure

lines.speaker: BERNARDO
lines.text: Comes armed through our watch; so like the king

lines.speaker: BERNARDO
lines.text: That was and is the question of these wars.

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: A mote it is to trouble the mind’s eye.

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: In the most high and palmy state of Rome,

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: A little ere the mightiest Julius fell,

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: The graves stood tenantless and the sheeted dead

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: Did squeak and gibber in the Roman streets:

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: As stars with trains of fire and dews of blood,

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: Disasters in the sun; and the moist star

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: Upon whose influence Neptune’s empire stands

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: Was sick almost to doomsday with eclipse:

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: And even the like precurse of fierce events,

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: As harbingers preceding still the fates

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: And prologue to the omen coming on,

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: Have heaven and earth together demonstrated

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: Unto our climatures and countrymen.—

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: But soft, behold! lo, where it comes again!

lines.speaker: MARCELLUS
lines.text: Shall I strike at it with my partisan?

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: Do, if it will not stand.

lines.speaker: BERNARDO
lines.text: ’Tis here!

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: ’Tis here!

lines.speaker: MARCELLUS
lines.text: ’Tis gone!

lines.speaker: MARCELLUS
lines.text: We do it wrong, being so majestical,

lines.speaker: MARCELLUS
lines.text: To offer it the show of violence;

lines.speaker: MARCELLUS
lines.text: For it is, as the air, invulnerable,

lines.speaker: MARCELLUS
lines.text: And our vain blows malicious mockery.

lines.speaker: BERNARDO
lines.text: It was about to speak, when the cock crew.

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: And then it started like a guilty thing

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: Upon a fearful summons. I have heard,

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: The cock, that is the trumpet to the morn,

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: Doth with his lofty and shrill-sounding throat

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: Awake the god of day; and, at his warning,

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: Whether in sea or fire, in earth or air,

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: The extravagant and erring spirit hies

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: To his confine: and of the truth herein

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: This present object made probation.

lines.speaker: MARCELLUS
lines.text: It faded on the crowing of the cock.

lines.speaker: MARCELLUS
lines.text: Some say that ever ’gainst that season comes

lines.speaker: MARCELLUS
lines.text: Wherein our Saviour’s birth is celebrated,

lines.speaker: MARCELLUS
lines.text: The bird of dawning singeth all night long:

lines.speaker: MARCELLUS
lines.text: And then, they say, no spirit dare stir abroad;

lines.speaker: MARCELLUS
lines.text: The nights are wholesome; then no planets strike,

lines.speaker: MARCELLUS
lines.text: No fairy takes, nor witch hath power to charm,

lines.speaker: MARCELLUS
lines.text: So hallow’d and so gracious is the time.

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: So have I heard and do in part believe it.

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: But, look, the morn, in russet mantle clad,

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: Walks o’er the dew of yon high eastward hill:

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: Break we our watch up; and by my advice,

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: Let us impart what we have seen to-night

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: Unto young Hamlet; for, upon my life,

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: This spirit, dumb to us, will speak to him.

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: Do you consent we shall acquaint him with it,

lines.speaker: HORATIO
lines.text: As needful in our loves, fitting our duty?

lines.speaker: MARCELLUS
lines.text: Let’s do’t, I pray; and I this morning know

lines.speaker: MARCELLUS
lines.text: Where we shall find him most conveniently.

stage_directions.text: Francisco at his post. Enter to him Bernardo.

I’ll cross it, though it blast me. Stay, illusion! If thou hast any sound, or use of voice, Speak to me: If there be any good thing to be done, That may to thee do ease and grace to me, Speak to me: Cock crows. If thou art privy to thy country’s fate, Which, happily, foreknowing may avoid, O, speak! Or if thou hast uphoarded in thy life Extorted treasure in the womb of earth, For which, they say, you spirits oft walk in death, Speak of it: stay, and speak! Stop it, Marcellus.

glosses.definition: loyal subjects to the King of Denmark
confirm what our eyes have seen
address the ghost (necessary to learn its purpose)
attack (surprise) your ears with our tale
you are educated (scholars knew Latin, the tongue for addressing spirits)
address the ghost (Horatio's Latin learning makes him fit)
wrongfully take over the night hours
the royal Denmark (King Hamlet) who is buried
struck the Poles on their sleds (a past victory)

glosses.phrase: foreign mart for implements of war

glosses.definition: gathered up rapaciously (like a shark feeds)
the realm of the sea-god (the oceans)

glosses.phrase: sick almost to doomsday with eclipse
glosses.definition: darkened as if the Last Judgment were coming

annotations.title: The exchange that opens a tragedy
annotations.body: Shakespeare begins not with a king or a murder but with two frightened sentries challenging each other in the dark. The first line — 'Who's there?' — is the question the entire play will ask of ghosts, of fathers, of the self. Critics since Maynard Mack have noted that Hamlet is built on a structure of interrogation: every major scene poses a question and refuses an easy answer. The sentries' midnight, bitter-cold, and 'sick at heart' place us immediately in a world where something has gone wrong before the play began. This is the formal opposite of Shakespeare's comedies, which typically begin at court in daylight with a stated problem to be solved.

annotations.title: Armed ghosts and Elizabethan belief
annotations.body: Horatio is called to speak to the ghost because he is 'a scholar' — in Elizabethan belief, Latin was the language that bound or banished spirits, and only educated men knew it. The ghost appears in full armor, 'cap-a-pe,' which was the convention of revenant kings: the armor that killed the rival Fortinbras is the armor the ghost wears. The Protestant reforms had officially abolished purgatory, and with it the theological place from which a ghost could lawfully return. Belief persisted, however, and Elizabethan accounts distinguished between holy spirits (truthful), damned goblins (lying), and hallucinations (worthless). The question 'Is thy soul damned?' will organize the first three acts.
sources: Keith Thomas, Religion and the Decline of Magic (1971)

annotations.title: The political backstory and Fortinbras
annotations.body: Horatio's long speech establishes the political frame: old Hamlet once killed old Fortinbras in single combat over territory, gaining lands for Denmark; now young Fortinbras is mustering an army to reclaim them. The audience will not meet Fortinbras until Act 4, but he shadows every scene — the son who acts decisively to avenge his father, against whom Hamlet is implicitly measured. The 'seal'd compact, well ratified by law and heraldry' signals that Shakespeare's Denmark observes chivalric rules; Claudius's later murder by poison is thus doubly monstrous, not just fratricide but a violation of the code that gave his predecessor his crown.

annotations.title: The Roman auspices and a world out of joint
annotations.body: Horatio invokes the portents before Julius Caesar's assassination — graves opening, comets, the moon eclipsed — to suggest that the ghost is such a warning for Denmark. The comparison is precise: both kings were killed by men close to them; both murders ushered in civil catastrophe; both produced sons who delayed their revenge. Shakespeare had staged Julius Caesar the year before Hamlet (1599), and audiences would hear the echo. The image of the dead returning as omens of political collapse sits at the heart of both plays: private crimes become public signs, and the state begins to hemorrhage meaning.
sources: Jonathan Bate, Soul of the Age (2008)

annotations.title: The 'season wherein our Saviour's birth is celebrated'
annotations.body: Marcellus's lines about Christmas driving away spirits preserve a pre-Reformation folk belief that Shakespeare's audience still half-held. The speech's beauty — 'no spirit dare stir abroad' — is also its theological retreat: Horatio replies 'in part I do believe it,' registering the Protestant intellectual's partial dissent. Shakespeare places this devotional lyric just before daybreak, allowing the scene's dread to thin into tenderness. The tonal shift prepares the audience for Hamlet's more skeptical voice in the following scene, and for the play's unresolved tension between a Catholic cosmology that gives the ghost meaning and a Protestant one that would deny it.
sources: Stephen Greenblatt, Will in the World (2004)

trials.prompt: What do Bernardo and Marcellus want Horatio to witness?
options: A military review of the night watch.
An apparition that has appeared to them on two previous nights.
The new king's first royal proclamation.
A strange comet passing over Elsinore.

trials.prompt: Why do the sentries want Horatio specifically to address the ghost?
options: He is the King's personal secretary and has royal authority.
As a scholar, he knows the Latin needed to address spirits properly.
He is Hamlet's closest friend and the ghost will speak only to him.
He has seen ghosts before and knows how to drive them off.

trials.prompt: What does Horatio recount to explain why Denmark is arming for war?
options: Claudius has declared a war of succession against Norway.
Young Fortinbras is assembling men to reclaim lands his father lost to King Hamlet.
A Polish invasion is expected before spring.
The English king has refused to pay his long-standing tribute.

trials.prompt: What function do Horatio's references to 'the mightiest Julius' and the omens before his assassination serve?
options: They establish Hamlet's Roman education.
They signal that the ghost is a political warning of coming catastrophe.
They suggest Horatio is a superstitious man whom we should not trust.
They foreshadow Hamlet's own death by stabbing.

trials.prompt: Why does Marcellus describe the Christmas season as a time when 'no spirit dare stir abroad'?
options: It is a reference to the cold weather driving ghosts indoors.
He invokes a folk belief that the holiness of Christmas banishes wandering spirits.
He is quoting a Latin liturgy for the feast of the Nativity.
He is reassuring Horatio that the ghost cannot harm them in December.
